claims.evidence_text: The optimal planting window for winter wheat in South Dakota is September 10 through October 10
claims.claim_summary: Optimal winter wheat planting window in South Dakota is September 10 to October 10

claims.evidence_text: Research from western South Dakota has shown that grain yield is decreased and that the crop typically suffers substantial winter injury when planting occurs later than October 15
claims.claim_summary: Planting winter wheat later than October 15 decreases grain yield and causes substantial winter injury

claims.evidence_text: Winter wheat should be planted at a rate of 960,000 pure-live-seeds per acre (22 pure-live-seeds per square foot) between September 10 and October 10.
claims.claim_summary: Recommended winter wheat seeding rate is 960,000 pure-live-seeds per acre (22 per square foot)

claims.evidence_text: Increase seeding rate to approximately 28 pure-live seeds per square foot under dry conditions to account for poor seedling germination and seed mortality.
claims.claim_summary: Increase winter wheat seeding rate to ~28 pure-live seeds per square foot under dry conditions or late planting
claims.management_system: winter wheat, South Dakota, dry/late conditions

claims.evidence_text: Under optimum conditions, growers should plant winter wheat at a depth of 1½ to 2 inches into a firm seedbed. Planting deeper than two inches delays emergence
claims.claim_summary: Optimum winter wheat seeding depth is 1.5 to 2 inches into a firm seedbed

claims.evidence_text: Depending on seeding rates, optimum plant stands are 20–28 plants per square foot.
claims.claim_summary: Optimum winter wheat plant stand is 20-28 plants per square foot

claims.evidence_text: Winter wheat also requires a period of exposure to near freezing temperatures (vernalization) to trigger reproductive development.
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat requires exposure to near-freezing temperatures (vernalization) to trigger reproductive development

claims.evidence_text: During vernalization, wheat plants are exposed to near-freezing temperatures (near 400 F is optimum).
claims.claim_summary: Optimum vernalization temperature for winter wheat is near 40°F

claims.evidence_text: For winter wheat to fully harden, a temperature below 500 F at the crown depth for 4 to 8 weeks is required.
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat requires temperature below 50°F at crown depth for 4 to 8 weeks to fully harden (cold acclimation)

claims.evidence_text: Generally 8 to 12 weeks of growth under field conditions are required for full development of winter hardiness.
claims.claim_summary: 8 to 12 weeks of field growth required for full development of winter hardiness in winter wheat

claims.evidence_text: Even fully hardened wheat plants can suffer winterkill, if temperatures drop below a crown temperature of 50° F.
claims.claim_summary: Fully hardened winter wheat can suffer winterkill if crown temperature drops below -50°F (stated as 50°F, likely -50°F)

claims.evidence_text: Plants at the reproductive stages (late boot to heading) are most sensitive to freeze injury (Table 4.1). At this stage, temperatures slightly below freezing can severely injure wheat, causing considerable yield reduction.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat at reproductive stages (late boot to heading) is most sensitive to spring freeze injury

claims.evidence_text: Tillering 12° F Leaf chlorosis; burning leaf tips; silage odor Slight to moderate
claims.claim_summary: At tillering stage, 12°F for 2 hours causes freeze injury (leaf chlorosis, burnt tips); slight-to-moderate yield impact

claims.evidence_text: Jointing 24° F Death of growing point; leaf yellowing or burning; lesions, odor Moderate to severe
claims.claim_summary: At jointing stage, 24°F for 2 hours causes freeze injury (death of growing point); moderate-to-severe yield impact

claims.evidence_text: Boot 28° F Floret sterility; spike trapped in the boot; damage to lower stem; leaf discoloration, odor Moderate to severe
claims.claim_summary: At boot stage, 28°F for 2 hours causes freeze injury (floret sterility); moderate-to-severe yield impact

claims.evidence_text: Heading 30° F Floret sterility; white awns or spikes; damage to lower stem; leaf discoloration Severe
claims.claim_summary: At heading stage, 30°F for 2 hours causes severe freeze injury (floret sterility, white awns)

claims.evidence_text: Flowering 30° F Floret sterility; white awns or spikes; damage to lower stem; leaf discoloration Severe
claims.claim_summary: At flowering (anthesis) stage, 30°F for 2 hours causes severe freeze injury (floret sterility)

claims.evidence_text: Milk 28° F White awns or white spikes; damage to lower stem; leaf discoloration; shrunken; roughened, or discolored kernels Moderate to severe
claims.claim_summary: At milk stage, 28°F for 2 hours causes freeze injury (shrunken/discolored kernels); moderate-to-severe yield impact

claims.evidence_text: Dough 28° F Shriveled, discolored kernels; poor germination Slight to moderate
claims.claim_summary: At dough stage, 28°F for 2 hours causes freeze injury (shriveled kernels, poor germination); slight-to-moderate yield impact

claims.evidence_text: Direct seeding winter wheat into standing stubble is recommended to reduce the risk of winterkill.
claims.claim_summary: Direct seeding winter wheat into standing stubble (no-till) is recommended to reduce winterkill risk

claims.evidence_text: Research conducted in western South Dakota has shown that soil temperature at crown depth under stubble can be 5 to 7 degrees warmer than under conventional fallow; these differences in temperature can influence winter survival of wheat seedlings.
claims.claim_summary: Soil temperature at crown depth under stubble is 5-7 degrees warmer than conventional fallow, improving winter survival

claims.evidence_text: Seeding winter wheat into broadleaf crop stubble is recommended to reduce insect, disease and weed problems.
claims.claim_summary: Seeding winter wheat into broadleaf crop stubble is recommended to reduce insect, disease and weed problems
